title: Don't Make Me Think
excerpt: A brilliantly concise guide to web usability built on the core principle that good websites let users accomplish their goals without conscious effort.
keyIdeas: The first law of usability: don't make me think — the page should be self-evident
Users don't read pages — they scan them, looking for what they need
Web designers waste time arguing over things that don't matter to users
Kill the welcome mat — remove introductory content that gets in the way
Usability testing with three users is better than testing with none
The user's tolerance for unusable sites is remarkably low
keyTakeaways: Design for scanning, not reading — use clear headings, short paragraphs, and visual hierarchy
Eliminate unnecessary words and choices to reduce cognitive load
Conventions are your friend — don't reinvent the wheel
Test early and often — one user test is worth a dozen expert opinions
Make every page self-explanatory so users don't have to think
whoShouldRead: Web designers and developers building websites or apps
Product managers who want to understand usability basics
Content creators writing for the web
Anyone who has ever watched a user struggle with a website
Startup founders building their first product
whoShouldSkip: Experienced UX professionals (the concepts are foundational)
Designers looking for advanced interaction design patterns
Those seeking visual design guidance or aesthetic principles
